questions.text: The Ring of Fire is located around which ocean?
questions.explanation: The Ring of Fire is a horseshoe-shaped zone around the Pacific Ocean, ~40,000 km in length. It accounts for about 90% of the world's earthquakes and ~75% of active volcanoes. It includes the coasts of Chile, USA (Alaska), Japan, Philippines, and Indonesia.

questions.text: India's only active volcano is located in:
questions.explanation: Barren Island in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands is India's only active volcano. It erupts periodically and is monitored by the Geological Survey of India. Narcondam Island (also in Andaman) is a dormant volcano.

questions.text: What does the Richter Scale measure?
options: Intensity of earthquake effects at a location
questions.explanation: The Richter Scale measures the magnitude (energy released) of an earthquake at its source. It is logarithmic — each step represents 10× the amplitude and ~31× the energy. The Modified Mercalli Scale (MMI) measures intensity — how much shaking is felt at a specific location.

questions.text: What is the point on Earth's surface directly above the focus of an earthquake called?
questions.explanation: The epicentre is the point on Earth's surface directly above the focus (hypocenter) — where the earthquake originates underground. Ground shaking and damage are typically most intense at or near the epicentre.

questions.text: P-waves (Primary seismic waves) differ from S-waves (Secondary seismic waves) in that P-waves:
options: Travel through both solid and liquid material
questions.explanation: P-waves (compressional waves) can travel through both solid and liquid. S-waves (shear waves) can only travel through solid material. The fact that S-waves don't pass through Earth's outer core proved it is liquid. P-waves are faster than S-waves.

questions.text: Which type of tectonic plate boundary creates ocean trenches and island arcs?
questions.explanation: At convergent boundaries, when oceanic plates collide, one subducts under the other creating ocean trenches (e.g., Mariana Trench) and volcanic island arcs (e.g., Japan, Philippines). When oceanic meets continental, fold mountains form (e.g., Andes).

questions.text: The 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami was triggered by an earthquake near:
options: Off the coast of Sumatra, Indonesia
questions.explanation: The December 26, 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami was triggered by a 9.1 magnitude earthquake off the northwest coast of Sumatra, Indonesia (near Aceh province). The resulting tsunami killed ~227,000 people across 14 countries.

questions.text: Which is the world's largest recorded earthquake by magnitude?
questions.explanation: The 1960 Valdivia earthquake in Chile (9.5 Mw) is the largest earthquake ever recorded by seismographs. The 2004 Sumatra earthquake (9.1) caused the deadliest tsunami. Haiti 2010 (7.0) was one of the deadliest by death toll due to shallow depth and proximity to Port-au-Prince.

questions.text: Which Indian earthquake prompted revision of India's seismic zoning map?
questions.explanation: The Bhuj earthquake (January 26, 2001) in Gujarat (7.7 magnitude, ~20,000 deaths) was a major wake-up call. Gujarat's Kutch region was upgraded to Seismic Zone V (highest) in the revised zoning map. The earthquake occurred on Republic Day, causing massive casualties.

questions.text: Vesuvius eruption in 79 AD buried which ancient Roman city?
questions.explanation: The 79 AD eruption of Mount Vesuvius buried the Roman city of Pompeii under ~4–6 m of volcanic ash and pumice, preserving it remarkably well. Herculaneum was also buried, but Pompeii is the more famous example and more commonly tested.

questions.text: What type of landslide hazard is particularly associated with Himalayan rivers during monsoon?
options: Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and debris flows
questions.explanation: Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) occur when glacial lakes (formed by melting glaciers) suddenly break through moraines, sending catastrophic floods downstream. The 2013 Kedarnath disaster in Uttarakhand combined GLOF, cloudbursts, and landslides, killing ~5,000+ people.

questions.text: The continental drift theory was proposed by which scientist?
questions.explanation: Alfred Wegener, a German meteorologist and geophysicist, proposed the Continental Drift Theory in 1912. He noted the jigsaw-fit of continents and matching fossils across oceans. His theory was later supported by evidence of seafloor spreading (Harry Hess, 1960s).

questions.text: The 'Bhuj Earthquake' of 2001 occurred in which Indian state?
questions.explanation: The Bhuj earthquake (January 26, 2001) occurred in the Kutch district of Gujarat, India. With a magnitude of 7.7, it killed approximately 20,000 people and injured over 1,60,000. It remains one of India's deadliest modern earthquakes.

questions.text: What is the correct classification of Mount Fuji (Japan)?
questions.explanation: Mount Fuji (3,776 m) is a dormant stratovolcano — it last erupted in 1707 (Hōei eruption). It is the iconic composite cone-shaped volcano of Japan, classified as dormant (not erupting recently but capable of future eruption). It is Japan's highest mountain.

questions.text: A tsunami is primarily caused by:
options: Submarine earthquake, volcanic eruption, or landslide
questions.explanation: Tsunamis are caused by sudden, large displacements of the ocean floor — most commonly by submarine earthquakes at subduction zones. Submarine volcanic eruptions and landslides can also trigger them. They travel at ~700–900 km/h in deep water but slow down and grow enormously tall near shore.

questions.text: Which seismic scale measures the INTENSITY (felt effects) of an earthquake at a particular location, rather than its magnitude?
questions.explanation: The Modified Mercalli Intensity Scale (MMI) measures the intensity of shaking experienced at a specific location (I = not felt, XII = total destruction). The Richter and Moment Magnitude scales measure the magnitude (total energy released) at the source. The Beaufort Scale measures wind speed.
